role_hints.oews_basis: 41-3041 Travel Agents 19.8% of wage bill (24.5% of employment)
role_hints.approx_share_of_wage_bill: ~20%; note many leisure advisors are 1099 independent contractors under host agencies and sit outside the W-2 wage bill
role_hints.note: Itinerary research, fare/rate searching, booking execution, rebooking and disruption handling are squarely within current AI capability; the persistent slice is trusted-advisor curation for complex/luxury/group trips and supplier-relationship leverage. The run should split transactional vs advisory work when setting the fraction.

role_hints.role: Reservation, ticketing and customer service staff
role_hints.oews_basis: 43-4181 Reservation and Transportation Ticket Agents 6.3% + 43-4051 Customer Service Representatives 4.9%
role_hints.note: Fulfillment and servicing work — queue management, ticketing, schedule changes — is the most directly automatable line; large TMCs are already automating it.
Supplier negotiations and client account management persist; ops reporting automates.

role_hints.oews_basis: 41-3091 Sales Representatives of Services 4.6% + 13-1161 Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists 3.9%
role_hints.note: Corporate-account selling is relationship work; marketing content and campaign ops automate heavily.

role_hints.oews_basis: 15-1252 Software Developers 5.7% (concentrated in OTAs and TMC platforms, not in retail agencies)
role_hints.note: A signature of the code's heterogeneity: the developer share belongs to the OTA/TMC-platform tier, not the small-agency band the screen targets.

role_hints.oews_basis: 43-1011 First-Line Supervisors of Office Workers 2.8% + 43-9061 Office Clerks 1.1% + long tail, est. remainder
role_hints.note: Invoicing, commission tracking/reconciliation (a notoriously manual task in host agencies), and document handling are near-fully automatable.

source_hints.source: Phocuswright research (US travel agency distribution and traveler technology studies)
source_hints.why: The standard travel-distribution research house; tracks channel shift, agency volumes, and AI-planning adoption by travelers and agencies.
Long-running agency-side survey covering technology use, revenue mix, and advisor demographics.

source_hints.source: ASTA (American Society of Travel Advisors) member research
source_hints.why: Advisor age, IC-vs-employee status, and agency ownership data for succession evidence.

source_hints.source: ARC (Airlines Reporting Corporation) agency sales data
source_hints.why: Hard data on accredited-agency air transaction volumes — evidences whether intermediated volume is growing or melting; air commissions are already near zero, a completed case study of supplier capture.

source_hints.source: Host Agency Reviews annual travel advisor income/fee surveys
source_hints.why: Evidence on service-fee adoption vs pure supplier-commission reliance among the small-agency tier the band targets.

source_hints.source: Travel Weekly / Phocuswire M&A coverage of host-agency and TMC consolidation (e.g. World Travel Holdings, Internova, TBO/host network deals)
source_hints.why: Deal activity in the tiers adjacent to the $1-10M band; disclosed multiples are scarce — expect commentary-level evidence.

capture_questions: Agency revenue is mostly supplier-paid commission (cruise/tour ~10-16%, hotel ~10%, air near zero) plus modest client service fees — if AI cuts an agency's fulfillment labor, the commission percentage is untouched and the saving is operator margin; but the controlling question is upstream: do suppliers keep paying commissions at current rates once AI booking channels let them acquire the same customer directly, or does the air-commission precedent (cut to zero in the early 2000s) repeat in cruise and hotel?

Who owns the client: leisure books are personal to the advisor, and a large share of advisors are 1099 ICs affiliated with host agencies who can re-affiliate freely — in an acquisition of an agency or host network, what actually transfers, and what does advisor churn do to it?

What stops the client from bypassing the agency with consumer AI trip-planners plus direct booking — and on the corporate side, do duty-of-care obligations, policy enforcement, negotiated rates and expense integration keep TMC contracts mandatory even when AI does the booking?

Gatekeepers: GDS contracts, ARC/IATA accreditation, and consortia (Virtuoso, Signature, Ensemble) hold the negotiated commission tiers and supplier relationships — does AI-era value accrue to those upstream aggregators rather than to the individual agency an acquirer would buy?
terminal_value_question: This industry has already been melted once: online self-serve booking eliminated the commodity retail agency in the 2000s, and the survivors are complexity niches — cruise/luxury/group leisure advisors and corporate managed travel. The mature-AI question is whether those niches survive a booking agent that can search, compare, book, and rebook directly with suppliers: specifically (a) do suppliers keep paying intermediary commissions when AI channels let them go direct (the air-commission zero-out is the historical mechanism to cite), and (b) does corporate duty-of-care/compliance keep TMC intermediation contractually required? Distinguish AI cutting an agency's fulfillment costs (margin gain on unchanged commissions) from AI becoming the booking intermediary (product replacement) — a diligent researcher can get this wrong by scoring the first and missing the second.
special_notes: Severe heterogeneity: 561510 spans mega-OTAs and corporate TMCs (48 firms in the 500+ class averaging $559M revenue — they dominate the $35.6B receipts) down to 6,236 micro leisure agencies. The $1-10M EBITDA band (n_band 250: the 20-99 and 100-499 classes) is mid-size TMCs, host agencies and niche specialists — score that population, not Expedia and not the solo advisor; set heterogeneous thinking accordingly.

Gross-vs-net revenue trap: agencies variously report gross bookings vs net commission revenue — margins, revenue/employee and multiples are incomparable across sources unless the basis is stated; SUSB receipts for this code mix both conventions.

1099 host-agency structure: tens of thousands of independent advisors sit outside SUSB employment and QCEW wages — firm counts and the 0.2099 labor_share (IPS-based, HIGH quality) describe the employer tier only.
COVID distortion: 2020-22 series (including the 2022 receipts and IPS 2022 labor share in this record) sit on partial recovery — check 2023-24 levels before treating them as steady-state.

Boundary confusion: 561520 Tour Operators (package assembly, principal risk) and 561599/561591 (other reservation services, CVBs) are siblings; 'travel agency' surveys and market sizings frequently blend them — check coverage before quoting.
EBITDA normalization: owner-operator comp dominates at the small end; host-agency P&Ls are thin-spread intermediary books where commission-split changes swing margins.
